perturbation.original_text: If requested, either Party shall be bound to return any and all materials to the Requesting Party within ____ days.
perturbation.changed_text: If requested, either Party shall be bound to return any and all materials to the Requesting Party within 1 day.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return period to 1 day may be unreasonably short and conflict with the Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which requires a reasonable time for compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act indicates that the remedy should be appropriate. Requiring someone to return all confidential information within 1 day may be seen as too short a time to be considered appropriate, especially if there's a substantial amount of data.

F.S. 688.003 688.003 Injunctive relief. — (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History. — s. 3, ch. 88-254.
perturbation.justification: The changed text sets a deadline of '1 day' for returning materials, which the LLM Law Explanation suggests may be unreasonably short and inappropriate under the Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Florida Statute 688.003, allows for injunctive relief in cases of misappropriation of trade secrets and allows courts to compel affirmative acts to protect a trade secret. Although the statute does not explicitly define what constitutes a reasonable time, setting an unreasonably short timeframe directly undermines the ability to comply with court-ordered protections of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: The bounded Party’s(ies’) duty to hold the Confidential Information in confidence shall remain in effect until such information no longer qualifies as a trade secret or written notice is given releasing such Party from this Agreem ent.
perturbation.changed_text: The bounded Party’s(ies’) duty to hold the Confidential Information in confidence shall remain in effect for 6 months, regardless of whether the information no longer qualifies as a trade secret.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the confidentiality period to 6 months, even if the information remains a trade secret, could violate the Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act allows for injunctive relief and damages for misappropriation of trade secrets. By limiting the confidentiality period to 6 months, the agreement effectively allows for the misappropriation of trade secrets after that period, even if they still qualify as trade secrets, contradicting the Act.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 002 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 002 Definitions. F.S. 688.002 688.002 Definitions. — As used in ss. 688.001 - 688.009 , unless the context requires otherwise: (1) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) “Misappropriation” means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that her or his knowledge of the trade secret was: a. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; b. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or c. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of her or his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) “Trade secret” means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. History. — s. 2, ch. 88-254; s. 750, ch. 97-102.
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the confidentiality period to 6 months, regardless of whether the information remains a trade secret. This contradicts the Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act (FUTSA), which protects trade secrets as long as they maintain their secrecy and provide economic value. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines a 'trade secret' in Florida Statute 688.002(4) as information that 'Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known... and Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy.' The modification allows for misappropriation after 6 months, even if the information still qualifies as a trade secret under FUTSA, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, this Agreement is created for the purpose of preventing the unauthorized disclosure of the confidential and proprietary information . The Parties agree as follows:
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, this Agreement is created for the purpose of preventing the unauthorized disclosure of the confidential and proprietary information. The Parties agree as follows: Any disputes arising out of this agreement shall be settled through binding arbitration within 30 days.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring binding arbitration within 30 days of a dispute arising could be problematic under Florida law if it unduly restricts access to courts or doesn't allow sufficient time for parties to understand their rights, or seek legal counsel.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Florida favors arbitration, the Florida Arbitration Code requires that the agreement to arbitrate be clear and unambiguous. Forcing arbitration within 30 days may not provide sufficient opportunity for parties to understand the implications and seek counsel, potentially rendering the agreement unconscionable.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 682 > Section 02 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 682 ARBITRATION CODE Entire Chapter SECTION 02 Arbitration agreements made valid, irrevocable, and enforceable; scope. F.S. 682.02 682.02 Arbitration agreements made valid, irrevocable, and enforceable; scope. — (1) An agreement contained in a record to submit to arbitration any existing or subsequent controversy arising between the parties to the agreement is valid, enforceable, and irrevocable except upon a ground that exists at law or in equity for the revocation of a contract. (2) The court shall decide whether an agreement to arbitrate exists or a controversy is subject to an agreement to arbitrate. (3) An arbitrator shall decide whether a condition precedent to arbitrability has been fulfilled and whether a contract containing a valid agreement to arbitrate is enforceable. (4) If a party to a judicial proceeding challenges the existence of, or claims that a controversy is not subject to, an agreement to arbitrate, the arbitration proceeding may continue pending final resolution of the issue by the court, unless the court otherwise orders. (5) This section also applies to written interlocal agreements under ss. 163.01 and 373.713 in which two or more parties agree to submit to arbitration any controversy between them concerning water use permit applications and other matters, regardless of whether or not the water management district with jurisdiction over the subject application is a party to the interlocal agreement or a participant in the arbitration. History. — s. 1, ch. 57-402; s. 12, ch. 67-254; s. 3, ch. 98-402; s. 26, ch. 2010-205; s. 7, ch. 2013-232. Note. — Former s. 57.11.
perturbation.justification: The added clause mandating binding arbitration within 30 days creates a potential legal contradiction under Florida law. Florida Statute 682.02(1) states that an agreement to arbitrate is valid, enforceable, and irrevocable "except upon a ground that exists at law or in equity for the revocation of a contract." The Law Explanation suggests that requiring arbitration within 30 days might not provide sufficient time for parties to understand the implications and seek counsel, potentially making the agreement unconscionable, which would be a ground for revocation. Therefore, the change introduces a potentially unenforceable term, contradicting the principle of valid and enforceable arbitration agreements if due process isn't adequately provided.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed under the laws in the State of Florida .
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed under the laws in the State of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware could be problematic if the agreement primarily involves Florida residents and business activities. Florida courts may refuse to enforce the choice of law provision if it violates a strong public policy of Florida.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 671.301(2) allows parties to choose the law that governs their agreement but this choice may not be enforced if it is contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 671 > Section 301 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 671 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE: GENERAL PROVISIONS Entire Chapter SECTION 301 Effective date; provision for transition; preservation of old transition provision. F.S. 671.301 671.301 Effective date; provision for transition; preservation of old transition provision. — (1) This act shall take effect at 12:01 a.m. on January 1, 1980. (2) Transactions validly entered into after January 1, 1967, and before the effective date of this act, and which were subject to the provisions of chapter 65-254, Laws of Florida, as amended, and which would be subject to this act as amended if they had been entered into after the effective date of this act and the rights, duties, and interests flowing from such transactions remain valid after the latter date and may be terminated, completed, consummated, or enforced as required or permitted by this act. Security interests arising out of such transactions which are perfected when this act becomes effective shall remain perfected until they lapse as provided in this act and may be continued as permitted by this act, except as stated in s. 680.108 , Florida Statutes 1979. (3) The provisions of s. 680.101 , Florida Statutes 1977, shall continue to apply to this act and for this purpose the two statutes shall be considered one continuous statute. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254; s. 1, ch. 69-218; s. 36, ch. 79-398; s. 6, ch. 90-278. Note. — Former s. 680.101.
perturbation.justification: The modification of the governing law from Florida to Delaware creates a potential contradiction with Florida law. Florida Statute 671.301(2), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, acknowledges parties' ability to choose governing law, but it also suggests that this choice may not be enforced if it goes against a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest. The Perturbation Explanation highlights the risk that Florida courts might refuse to enforce the Delaware choice of law if the agreement primarily involves Florida residents and business activities, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information also includes any and all, work products, studies and other material prepared by or in the possession or contro l of the other Party, which contain, include, refer to or otherwise reflect or are generated from any Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information also includes any and all, work products, studies and other material prepared by or in the possession or contro l of the other Party, which contain, include, refer to or otherwise reflect or are generated from any Confidential Information. This excludes source code, software, and other copyrightable works created by the second party.
perturbation.explanation: Excluding copyrightable works created by the second party might contradict Florida's trade secret laws if such works embody or disclose the first party's trade secrets. Trade secrets can be embedded in source code or other software.
perturbation.law_explanation: If the second party creates software that incorporates the first party's trade secrets, excluding the software from the definition of Confidential Information allows the second party to potentially misappropriate those trade secrets without violating the NDA, which conflicts with the protections afforded by the Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act.

F.S. 688.002 688.002 Definitions. — As used in ss. 688.001 - 688.009 , unless the context requires otherwise: (1) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) “Misappropriation” means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that her or his knowledge of the trade secret was: a. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; b. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or c. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of her or his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) “Trade secret” means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. History. — s. 2, ch. 88-254; s. 750, ch. 97-102.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text, by excluding copyrightable works created by the second party from the definition of "Confidential Information", creates a legal contradiction with Florida's trade secret laws. According to the Law Explanation, if the second party's software incorporates the first party's trade secrets, this exclusion allows for potential misappropriation of those trade secrets without violating the NDA, conflicting with the Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines "Misappropriation" as the acquisition or disclosure of a trade secret by improper means or without consent, and defines "Trade secret" broadly to include information like programs or methods that derive economic value from not being generally known and are subject to reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. By excluding copyrightable works, the contract weakens the protection of trade secrets embedded within those works, potentially facilitating misappropriation if the software embodies the first party's trade secrets.
